title: Cumulative number of objects launched into space
subtitle: This includes satellites, probes, landers, crewed spacecrafts, and space station flight elements launched into Earth orbit or beyond.
caption:
Cumulative number of objects launched into space

This includes satellites, probes, landers, crewed spacecrafts, and space station flight elements launched into Earth orbit or beyond.

The chart tracks the cumulative count of space objects from 1957 to 2025, with the vertical axis ranging from 0 to 25,000. The "World" total, represented by the top orange line, begins at zero in 1957 and rises steadily to approximately 5,000 by the year 2000. Following 2010, the slope of this line steepens significantly, crossing the 10,000 mark around 2020 and reaching the 25,000 limit by the end of the timeline. The United States, shown in brown, follows a similar trajectory but at a lower volume, starting near zero and reaching roughly 15,000 by 2025, with a sharp acceleration visible after 2015. Russia’s line, in blue, shows a more gradual increase, plateauing between 3,000 and 4,000 from 1990 through 2010 before rising slightly to just under 5,000 by 2025. China, depicted in purple, remains near zero until roughly 2005, after which it climbs to approximately 2,000 by 2025. The lines for Japan, France, India, Germany, and the European Space Agency remain clustered at the bottom of the chart, all staying below the 1,000 mark throughout the entire period. The data attributes objects launched by one country on behalf of another to the latter entity.
